item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i felt genuinely bad for the printer when it had its existantial crisis. he's just a cog in the research machine, but its all fake :( you made me empathize with a printer but you also made me laugh at how quickly the researchers decided to push the limits of how ridiculous it could get. you can have my +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As the first image hasn't been sourced and cannot be sourced, it is to be assumed it is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as has thus been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting scip with some cool ideas around consciousness and self worth but I just don't know if the printer being sentient is a good thing or not. It almost feels like waste of a good scip one way or the other. By that I mean I would have like a printer that attempts to solve evolutionary problems. I would also have liked a sentient piece of technology that comes to an awful realisation but I don't know if I like both in the same article. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Wait, why did the foundation even consider using an office chair?"

The chain of logic isn't that hard to follow: They know what it does with images of living things. This raises the obvious question "What it would do with an image of something that clearly was not a living thing?". One photo of a chair later and you have a test.

It could also be an unintentional test stemming from researchers goofing around with a safe SCP.

Foundation staff acting like irresponsible cartoon characters is called "lolFoundation". It's not a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes I know, necroposting and all that, but I have some constructive criticism (even though the author is probably gone by now). I feel like the jump from testing with actual animals and furniture is a bit nonsensical. I understand that the photos needed to be tied in, and it was tied in well with the entry. My issue is that as I read I thought, "Wait, why did the foundation even consider using an office chair?"

You could show that they used another item as sort of a link, like a biological material that isn't technically alive (maybe some kind of biofuel or something). It could also be an unintentional test stemming from researchers goofing around with a safe SCP.

Also, I wonder what would happen if the foundation was to input an extinct creature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be a perfect use. Although it would probably take till the heat death of the universe or something to calculate, or just print out chuthulu or some other Lovecraftian omnipotent god/entity that would cause an XK-Class end of the world scenario

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh… poor little… printer? This site does some really weird shit with my emotions. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lorem ipsum text is from an ancient roman text relating to morality?
since it was it was fed text relating to morality my interpretation is that it probably in turn make the printer evolve instead of printing it's evolution.
Sometimes natural evolution does not consider other factors (such as morality) and it doesn't make it untrue.

Upvote.
So far at the end the printer seems to have trouble deciding what is reality (perhaps a way of rationalizing what it do).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not often you can make me laugh and feel sad about laughing at the same time. The suicide-by-chair was a great "punch line" to the entire SCP. However, I think if you were to add more tests, showing how the printer became gradually more suicidal and building up more towards its suicide, it would be funnier and more realistic. Upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted because I wanted to give it the text version of a hug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me feel as an adult what 'The Brave Little Toaster' made me feel as a child. I kind of hate you for that.

And as someone else in the comments section said, I'd love to see the printer used in a tale or some experiments. For example, it'd be a great way to try and figure out how to deal with 682 without actually risking any mutations/breaches.

Oh yes, and upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me laugh and feel sad at the same time. +1

item.title: I am surprised.
item.content:encoded:
No discussion on the statement that it "can't ever get out"?

How does it know it can't? Get out of what? Does it mean its existence… or does it mean its printer ?

I love the whole thing, though. Even the typo in the chair's text was nice, after I read that the printer occasionally produced typos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna give it a hug and a cup of hot chocolate, and tell it that it will be ok. The story took a random turn from lolrandom to sadness in a few sentences, and that secured my upboat.

item.title: Image Restoration
item.content:encoded: Upon receiving permission from the owner, SCP-1324's output image has been restored.

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1324's secondary image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Fantem or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

Source:
http://www.kibardindesign.com/en/collection/collection7/angel-chair.aspx

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. It has only the vaguest idea of the logic, science and mechanics involved in the thing it thinks it exists to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you look in the comments further up? It was explained…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god, I didn't realize this until I read it. I am going back and upvoting this. Goddamnit, you should print this page and send it to a local psychology teacher.

(Great job, Foundation experimenter guy, you broke the poor printer.)

What if someone were to put in a load of text attempting to convince it that it still had worth?

And on that note, I can see this printer being used for greatness someday. Say a bioengineer were to put in tons of relevant data on an unbalanced ecosystem. The printer's output would determine the modifications the bioengineer introduced. Sure, it's not the same as being the "god of evolution", but it's close!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not. "A billion" is a different value in some cultures than it is in others, because the there was a mass change in the definition of it in some Western nations at some point.

In things like the military and scientific institutions where it's vitally important that everyone be on the same page about that sort of thing, they use a a standard. But everywhere else, it's a local thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh? I don't get what you mean. I thought standard form was the standard everywhere .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
o_o

That is perfect!
You've illustrated cognitive disonance better than any psych 101 professor ever could.
The printer knows that it was all a lie, that is why it is commiting suicide. But at the same time, all it has ever known is the lie, so it can't help but act as if the "facts" were true. True and false, both believed at the same time, without even realizing it.

I liked it before, now I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was confused about the ending until someone in the comments clarified it. Perhaps it is a little too muddled? Just my thoughts. Everything else was pretty darn fascinating. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…you…ok, that's fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you change the ending to the stool to make it not suicide? How is this suicide? I don't get it. It just seems to be a mental breakdown of sorts.

Anyway, this looked like your every day run of the mill SCP until the end, so I appreciate it for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation was that the printer doesn't exactly know the meaning of all the words it uses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The printer had previously been made aware of the aggressive, predatory properties possessed by furniture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This went from amusing, to interesting, to fascinating. I think that the idea of everything you've ever known turning out to be a lie is more than enough to make someone commit suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I was really gonna like this when I looked at the second picture, but honestly I was just a little underwhelmed. The ending felt too cliche, even though it kinda makes sense. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I am here to help you simulate the evolution"

Buh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything is nice and properly SCP-like 'til the end, which is not bad, but maybe a bit too earnest. Or maybe not. I'm kind of on the fence about whether it works with the rest of the piece or not but either way it strikes me as slightly…mushy? Not quite the right word, but close.

I'm also confused as to what I'm missing in that end bit. It certainly communicates despair, but for the life of me I can't see the suicide attempt people are talking about in the comments. Is it asking to be perched on a stool so it can dash itself to bits on the floor?

Anyway.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it's from an alternate universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would break my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the "working printer" bit is part of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one problem here — HP printers are not capable of functioning.

Otherwise nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, my reaction to the incident was "Why does it even need to be sentient?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it because of the end. Gave me chills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the end part, but I like the weirdness of it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, my bad, I'm in Canada and it's the same as US. I feel dumb for not knowing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To tell you the truth, I wouldn't be happy with this article terminating in anything but complete disillusionment. Regardless of the effect it might have to remove that, I would be unsatisfied for it and it drive me absolutely mad.

I may rewrite this, however, to make that culmination more natural and convincing. Until then, I'll just leave this is as a slightly more grand matter of "you can't please everyone".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it +1

I also felt sorry for the poor little printer. That's kind of a big drop down, from god to creative artist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most people don't commit suicide because a single thing goes wrong, however devastatingly wrong it might go;

Not if the single thing is as important to them as this was to the printer i.e. just about the sole thing they care about.
Also. … who knows whether it wasn't rationalizing shit like hunter-killer chairs all the time, and the piece of text with dummy input was the bit that made it finally click together?

Yes, I am aware that the article describes a 30-minute delay, and we don't know how fast it thinks so it's possible all sorts of things were going on in its head during the delay.

I for one think it went through some sort of a thought process like that. Besides, not like it'd consider the outside world reliable anymore at that point - it might well feel betrayed by whoever it thought it was getting the tasks from.

Whether this seems reasonable to anybody else is really none of my business. I'm just saying that from my particular perspective, I find the immediate jump to despair unlikely, melodramatic, and unconvincing.

And that's fine - in the same vein, I'm disagreeing with you because I liked that particular bit of the entry, and all in all, we are arguing in front of an audience who might well be swayed by one or another POV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just found it narmy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, and it's a sapient evolution-simulating printer, so saying the psychology is "unrealistic" seems like the wrong accusation anyway. Nevertheless.

While it's true that people have offed themselves for less, as far as I can tell the printer's internal, subjective life had been just peachy up until this point. Most people don't commit suicide because a single thing goes wrong, however devastatingly wrong it might go; generally there was already something going badly, and then the devastating thing pushes one over the edge. If things were already going fine, and then some surprising terrible thing happens, we have a robust array of psychological defense mechanisms to get through first.

The printer doesn't seem to reach out to other sources of information (and it should be able to infer that there's something else out there to communicate with, since it's getting input from somewhere), it doesn't try to rationalize that the simulations must have some worth even if they are only simulations, it doesn't burn with rage against the source of its input for lying to it all this time, it doesn't turn to the Printer God for solace, it doesn't become delusional in an attempt to find an alternate explanation for the input it received (for example, by deciding that perhaps some of the text is actually pictures of animals that just happen to look a great deal like nonsense text), etc.

Yes, I am aware that the article describes a 30-minute delay, and we don't know how fast it thinks so it's possible all sorts of things were going on in its head during the delay. Since it doesn't refer to any of that thought process in its suicide note, though, there's at least a weak indication that none of those were considered, and we know for sure that it didn't attempt to contact whoever was providing it with input.

Whether this seems reasonable to anybody else is really none of my business. I'm just saying that from my particular perspective, I find the immediate jump to despair unlikely, melodramatic, and unconvincing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it until the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unrealistic?
Everything the printer thought it was working for since who knows when has turned out to be false, a simulation.
People have offed themselves for less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it until Incident 1324-2. Then I hated it. There are plenty of emotional responses available to a sapient evolution-simulating printer besides suicidal despair. That's the part of this SCP that jumped out at me as being jarringly unrealistic, gods help me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second picture is running into the boxes, at least for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While that is correct, I later found out that the scientific definition of one billion is 1 x 109. So I'm made to go with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent article, +1 would vote again.
The "evolutions" were great, and the end note, well, poor printer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a billion anywhere not in USA, because 1e9 is a milliard there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would you feel if you found out you weren't the god of evolution? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, the printer realised that what was going on wasn't real. Prior to this, it had thought that the creatures it poorly simulated evolution were real, living creatures.

And then it came to its attention that its reality was an empty sham. So it tried to kill itself using the only information available to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand Incident 1324-2, can someone explain it to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The printer in that picture is identical to my printer. Not including the anomalous stuff, of course.

(It's a good article, too. +1.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
omg thanks for writing this. ;_;

Upboated

I come up with ideas but never implement them lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should make a note here: this is based on an idea BBritain graciously gave me. I'd like to thank him for the inspiration.
